segments.title: Define what the research must explain
segments.summary: Start archival research by deciding whether the project needs strategic, tactical, operational, personnel, or construction evidence.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke uses a study of cruisers to show that a broad subject has to be narrowed into answerable research needs. Strategic reasons for building ships, tactical employment, operational practice, recruitment, and construction data each point toward different records. A project about why ships were built and how they operated does not automatically require the personal papers of every officer who served in them. The initial plan may change once an archive reveals new leads, but it gives the search a useful direction and prevents indiscriminate collecting.

evidence.note: The talk distinguishes strategic, tactical, operational, personnel, and construction questions.

segments.title: Choose evidence for a story, fact, question, or theory
segments.summary: A search for personal stories needs different collections from a factual inquiry, while testing a theory requires a wider net.
segments.body: The talk separates personal papers and narrative accounts from national and institutional records. A researcher seeking an individual story may need private papers or specialist libraries, while a factual investigation may begin with government collections such as the National Archives. Dr. Clarke also cautions that testing a theory normally requires broader evidence than answering a tightly framed question. Defining that purpose early therefore helps determine both the repositories to consult and the scale of the search.

evidence.note: Dr. Clarke contrasts story and fact research, then narrow questions and theory testing.

segments.title: Use a strong book to map the sources
segments.summary: A well-sourced scholarly book can lead researchers from its bibliography to staff histories, technical histories, and archive series.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke recommends beginning with a good academic work near the chosen subject, even when it is not an exact match. Its index, source list, and archival citations reveal useful records, published Admiralty staff histories, technical histories, and the repositories that hold them. He demonstrates how archive references such as ADM series numbers can be carried from a book into a catalogue search. Detailed bibliographic notes make the earlier scholar's route reproducible and turn secondary literature into a practical map of the primary material.

evidence.note: The transcript demonstrates following Friedman and other academic bibliographies into Admiralty and archive sources.

segments.title: Follow journal citations to primary material
segments.summary: Online journals provide another citation trail to the primary sources and repositories used by earlier researchers.
segments.body: The same method works when access begins with JSTOR or another scholarly journal rather than a book. Article notes and citations can identify primary documents, source collections, and the archives visited by earlier researchers. Following those references helps establish which repositories and record series are likely to matter before travel begins. Published scholarship therefore serves as a discovery tool and audit trail as well as background reading.

evidence.note: The speaker recommends tracing journal citations to primary sources and archive lists.

segments.title: Search catalogues broadly, then refine
segments.summary: A broad catalogue search for terms such as sloops can reveal National Archives records and holdings at other UK repositories.
segments.body: The demonstration begins with a broad keyword and date range, then uses catalogue filters to distinguish National Archives material from records held by other participating UK repositories. A search for sloops exposes designs, hydrographic records, photographs, logbooks, correspondence, and collections in institutions ranging from the British Library to local record offices. Some results are irrelevant, but unexpected hits reveal specialist archives that a researcher might otherwise miss. The object of the first search is to map what survives and where it is held before refining the enquiry.

evidence.note: A catalogue search for sloops illustrates date filters, other archives, and potentially relevant holdings.

segments.title: Read catalogue results for institutional clues
segments.summary: Catalogue entries can reveal which departments created relevant records, helping researchers refine a naval-history search beyond one obvious series.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke uses results to identify material created by the Admiralty, Colonial Office, Foreign Office, Treasury, and Board of Trade. Those institutional labels reveal that a naval question may also have diplomatic, colonial, financial, commercial, or labour dimensions. They provide new departments, date ranges, and record groups with which to refine the catalogue search. A result need not answer the question immediately to improve the next stage of the investigation.

evidence.note: The transcript explains using departmental origins and dates to refine a catalogue search.

segments.title: Plan document orders and archive visits
segments.summary: Once records are identified, account for digitisation limits, ordering rules, document caps, and the time needed for an archive visit.
segments.body: Some catalogue records have not been digitised and therefore require a visit or a requested copy. Dr. Clarke explains the practical distinction between a standard visit with a smaller document allowance and a bulk order drawn from one series. Booking limits and advance-order rules determine how much evidence can realistically be examined in a day or week. Estimating the number and location of documents before travelling turns a catalogue search into a workable research schedule.

evidence.note: The speaker discusses non-digitised records, visits, bulk orders, and booking limits.

segments.title: Contact smaller archives before travelling
segments.summary: A polite enquiry can uncover uncatalogued or recently deposited material and prevent an under-planned research trip.
segments.body: Smaller archives may rely heavily on volunteers, have incomplete online catalogues, or have received collections not yet fully listed. The talk recommends contacting staff courteously with the research topic and a known catalogue reference before travelling. Staff may be able to identify related deposits that do not yet appear online and explain local access arrangements. That conversation can reveal whether a day is enough, whether several days are needed, and how to plan the trip around the actual workload.

evidence.note: Dr. Clarke advises contacting archives about material beyond their online listings and planning travel around the likely workload.

segments.title: Adapt the research cycle to access rules
segments.summary: Advance booking and document limits can replace an iterative order-and-reread routine, so the research method must adjust to the archive's access system.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke contrasts his former National Archives routine with a system requiring documents and visits to be booked weeks ahead. Previously, he could revisit material from one session while ordering the next group of files in response to what he found. Advance booking weakens that rapid feedback loop and makes pre-visit catalogue work more important. The larger lesson is that an archival method must adapt to the institution's access rules rather than assuming every repository supports the same research rhythm.

evidence.note: The transcript contrasts an earlier iterative ordering routine with advance-booking restrictions and explains the need to adjust.

segments.title: Treat travel as part of the research budget
segments.summary: Document volume, transport, accommodation, and local advice determine whether a multi-day archive visit is affordable and productive.
segments.body: A distant archive visit is a logistical project as well as an intellectual one. Dr. Clarke advises estimating document volume early enough to reserve several consecutive days and avoid an expensive return trip. His examples from Liverpool, Newcastle, and Glasgow show that the closest hotel is not always the cheapest or easiest base once daily transport and equipment are considered. Archive staff can often offer candid local advice, while early booking can make a longer stay financially practical.

evidence.note: Dr. Clarke explains how document volume, repeat travel, accommodation location, and staff advice affect trip planning.

segments.title: Pack for practical archive work
segments.summary: Bring reliable note-taking and imaging equipment, backups, power, cash, and a pencil while respecting reading-room rules.
segments.body: The field kit described here includes a laptop or another legible note-taking method, a capable camera, chargers, a backup storage device, and cash for archives that still charge for services. Wide and close photographs may be essential where copying facilities are limited, so power and redundant storage matter throughout the day. A pencil is required because ink can permanently alter originals, while food and drink remain outside the reading room. Dr. Clarke also explains why older books may be inspected or excluded after past attempts to insert pages and falsify collections. These preparations protect the research record, comply with reading-room security, and safeguard the documents being consulted.

evidence.note: The final practical guidance covers equipment, backups, pencils, cameras, and reading-room restrictions.
